title: Reflective symmetry of 2D shapes
description: Analyze various shapes according to their reflective symmetries.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the parallelogram below?**


[[☃ image 1]]

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{CG}$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{DH}$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $\overline{CG}$

Under this transformation, some points on the parallelogram map to another point which is no longer on the parallelogram.  


[[☃ image 1]]

The parallelogram is *not symmetric* over this line.

##Reflection over $\overline{DH}$

Under this transformation, some points on the parallelogram map to another point which is no longer on the parallelogram.  


[[☃ image 1]]

The parallelogram is *not symmetric* over this line.

Neither symmetry applies to the parallelogram.

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{CG}$ | No
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{DH}$ | No

options.alt: A parallelogram with vertices at A, C ,G and E. Side A G has a midpoint H. Side C E has a midpoint D. Both sides A G and C E are congruent. Side A C has a midpoint B. Side G E has a midpoint F. Both sides A C and G E are congruent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the rectangle below?**


[[☃ image 1]]

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{BF}$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{AE}$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $\overline{BF}$

Under this transformation, each point on the rectangle maps to another point which is also on the rectangle.


[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle is *symmetric* over this line.

##Reflection over $\overline{AE}$

Under this transformation, some points on the rectangle map to another point which is no longer on the rectangle.  


[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle is *not symmetric* over this line.

The symmetries that apply to the rectangle are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{BF}$ | Yes
Reflective symmetry over $\overline{AE}$ | No

options.alt: A rectangle with vertices at A, C, G and E. Side A G has a midpoint H. Side C E has a midpoint D. Both sides A G and C E are congruent. Side A C has a midpoint B. Side G E has a midpoint F. Both sides A C and G E are congruent.

options.alt: A trapezoid with vertices at A, C, G and E. Side A G has a midpoint H. Side C E has a midpoint D. Both sides A C and G E are congruent. Side A C has a midpoint B. Side G E has a midpoint F.

assessment_items.question_content:
The center of the following regular nonagon is at the origin.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the nonagon?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-x$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflection symmetry over the line $y=-\dfrac23 x$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=-x$

Under this transformation, some points on the nonagon map to another point which is no longer on the nonagon. The nonagon is *not symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y=-\dfrac23 x$

This line goes through the center of the nonagon and one of the corner points $(6,-4)$.

Under this transformation, each point on the nonagon maps to another point which is also on the nonagon.  The nonagon is *symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the nonagon are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-x$ | No
Reflection symmetry over the line $y=-\dfrac23 x$ | Yes

options.alt: A nanogon mapped onto a coordinate plane. The center is at the origin. The nanogon has a point at zero point five, seven, a point at five, five, a point at seven, one, a point at six, negative four, a point at two, negative seven, a point at negative three, negative six point five, a point at negative six point five, negative three, a point at negative seven, two, and a point at negative four point two, six. The equations y equals negative two-thirds X and y equals negative X are both graphed on the coordinate plane. All points are estimates.

assessment_items.question_content:
The center of the following star is at the origin.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the star?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=x$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over the $y$-axis | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=x$

Under this transformation, some points on the star map to another point which is no longer on the star. The star is *not symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y$-axis

The $y$-axis goes through the center of the star and one of the corner points $(0,9)$. 

Under this transformation, each point on the star maps to another point which is also on the star. The star is *symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the star are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=x$ | No
Reflective symmetry over the $y$-axis | Yes

options.alt: A star mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The center is at the origin. The star has a point around zero, nine, a point around nine, three, a point around negative nine, three, a point around negative five, negative seven, and a point around five, negative seven. The equation y equals X is graphed on the coordinate plane. All points are estimates.

assessment_items.question_content:
The center of the following regular hexagon is at the origin.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the hexagon?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-\dfrac{7}{3}x$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over the $x$-axis | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=-\dfrac{7}{3}x$

This line goes through two of the opposite corner points $(-3, 7)$, and $(3,-7)$.

Under this transformation, each point on the hexagon maps to another point which is also on the hexagon. The hexagon is *symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $x$-axis

Under this transformation, some points on the hexagon map to another point which is no longer on the hexagon. The hexagon is *not symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the hexagon are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-\dfrac{7}{3}x$ | Yes
Reflective symmetry over the $x$-axis | No

options.alt: A hexagon mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The center is at the origin. The hexagon has a point around negative eight, one, a point around eight, negative  one, a point around negative three, nine, a point around three, negative seven, a point around five, six, and a point around negative five, negative six. The equation y equals negative seven over three X is graphed on the coordinate plane. All points are estimates.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows a kite.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the kite?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over $y=x$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over $y=-x-5$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=x$

This line goes through two of the opposite corner points $(8, 8)$ and $(-6,-6)$.

Under this transformation, each point on the kite maps to another point which is also on the kite. The kite is *symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y=-x-5$

Under this transformation, some points on the kite map to another point which is no longer on the kite. The kite is *not symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the kite are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=x$ | Yes
Reflection symmetry over the line $y=-x-5$ | No

options.alt: A kite mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The kite has a point at negative six, negative six, a point at negative five, zero, a point at zero, negative five, and a point at eight, eight. The equations y equals negative X minus five and y equals X are both graphed on the coordinate plane.

assessment_items.question_content:
The center of the following rhombus is at the origin.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the rhombus?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-2x$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=\dfrac{1}{2}x$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=-2x$

This line goes through the center of the rhombus and two of the corner points $(-2,4)$ and $(2,-4)$. 

Under this transformation, each point on the rhombus maps to another point which is also on the rhombus. The rhombus is *symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y=\dfrac{1}{2}x$

This line goes through the center of the rhombus and two of the corner points $(8,4)$ and $(-8,-4)$. 

Under this transformation, each point on the rhombus maps to another point which is also on the rhombus. The rhombus is *symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the rhombus are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-2x$ | Yes
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=\dfrac{1}{2}x$ | Yes

options.alt: A rhombus mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The center of the rhombus is at the origin. The rhombus has a point at negative eight, negative four, a point at negative two, four, a point at two, negative four, and a point at eight, four. The equations y equals negative two X  and y equals one half X are both graphed on the coordinate plane.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows a right triangle.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the triangle?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-x$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over the $x$-axis | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=-x$

Under this transformation, some points on the triangle map to another point which is no longer on the triangle. The triangle is *not symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over the $x$-axis

Under this transformation, some points on the triangle map to another point which is no longer on the triangle. The triangle is *not symmetric* over this line.


[[☃ image 1]]

Neither symmetry applies to the triangle.

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-x$ | No
Reflective symmetry over the $x$-axis | No

options.alt: A right triangle mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The right triangle has a point at negative eight, negative four, a point at four, negative four, and a point at four, six. The equation y equals negative X is graphed on the coordinate plane.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows an equilateral triangle.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the triangle?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $x=0$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-\dfrac{2}{3}x$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $x=0$

Under this transformation, some points on the triangle map to another point which is no longer on the triangle. The triangle is *not symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y=-\dfrac{2}{3}x$

This line goes through the center of the triangle and one of the corner points $(6,-4)$. 

Under this transformation, each point on the triangle maps to another point which is also on the triangle. The triangle is *symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the triangle are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $x=0$ | No
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-\dfrac{2}{3}x$ | Yes

options.alt: An equilateral triangle mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The equilateral triangle has a point at about negative six, negative three, a point at about one half, seven, and a point at six, negative four. The equation x equals 0 is graphed. The equation y equals negative two thirds x is graphed.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram shows a right triangle.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the triangle?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=4$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=\dfrac17x+3$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=4$

Under this transformation, some points on the triangle map to another point which is no longer on the triangle. The triangle is *not symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y=\dfrac{1}{7}x+3$

This line goes through one of the corner points, $(7,4)$, and the midpoint of the opposite side of the triangle. 

Under this transformation, each point on the triangle maps to another point which is also on the triangle. The triangle is *symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the triangle are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=4$ | No
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=\dfrac17x+3$ | Yes

options.alt: A right triangle mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The right triangle has a point at four, zero, a point at three, seven, and a point at seven, four. The equations y equals four and y equals one over seven X plus three are both graphed on the coordinate plane.

Reflective symmetry over the line $y=\dfrac{5}{3}x$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=\dfrac{3}{5}x$

Under this transformation, some points on the parallelogram map to another point which is no longer on the parallelogram. The parallelogram is *not symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y=\dfrac{5}{3}x$

Under this transformation, some points on the parallelogram map to another point which is no longer on the parallelogram. The parallelogram is *not symmetric* over this line.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows a trapezoid.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following reflective symmetries apply to the trapezoid?**

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-x$ | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-7x+1$ | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: **Reflective (or line) symmetry** means that we can reflect a figure across a line, and the figure will remain unchanged.

##Reflection over $y=-x$

This line goes through the midpoints of the parallel sides and the center of the trapezoid. 

Under this transformation, each point on the trapezoid maps to another point which is also on the trapezoid. The trapezoid is *symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

##Reflection over $y=-7x+1$

Under this transformation, some points on the trapezoid map to another point which is no longer on the trapezoid. The trapezoid is *not symmetric* over this line.



[[☃ image 1]]

The symmetries that apply to the trapezoid are:

Symmetry | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-x$ | Yes
Reflective symmetry over the line $y=-7x+1$ | No

options.alt: A trapezoid mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The trapezoid has a point at negative eight, one, a point at negative one, eight, a point at six, negative one, and a point at one negative six. The equations y equals negative X  and y equals negative seven X plus one are both graphed on the coordinate plane.
